hero.tagline: Standard $7,000 cap and 7-day appeal window for magistrate decisions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Only if heard by a magistrate

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $7,000, you must either waive the excess amount to file in small claims court or file in a higher court. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to fit under the cap is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan allows you 6 years to file claims on written contracts and 6 years on oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $1,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement and any partial payments.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $2,500 with the understanding they'd repay in 6 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan terms and any communications regarding repayment.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 and missed payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $3,000 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Keep copies of the invoice, service agreement, and any related correspondence.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Maintain detailed account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Send a 30-day demand letter before filing. You may recover the check amount plus damages.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Show the co-signed agreement and evidence of payments made.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan provides a 6-year statute of limitations for most consumer-related claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Keep the receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Document the service agreement and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue and caused further damage.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Obtain repair records, receipts, and expert evaluations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory contractor work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $5,000 for home renovations but left the job incomplete and subpar.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from contract date. Gather the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,200 for an event, but they never showed up.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from event date. Provide the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continued to charge $50 monthly after you canceled the subscription.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from charge date. Keep records of cancellation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $6,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Retain the sales contract, warranty, and repair records.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A product advertised as 'all-natural' contained synthetic ingredients, and you paid $200 based on the false claim.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Save the advertisement and purchase receipt.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan allows 3 years to file claims for property damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,500 in damage to your car and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Obtain police reports, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,000 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Document the damage with photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $3,500 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection reports.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed through your $1,200 patio furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Take photos and gather repair receipts.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 watch with a jeweler for repair, and they damaged it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage. Keep the service agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan provides specific statutes for landlord-tenant disputes, with a 4-year statute of limitations for security deposit claims.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection reports.

claims.name: Landlord didn't provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord kept $800 of your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Request the itemized list in writing before filing.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from discovery. Document the issues with photos and maintenance requests.

claims.name: Landlord kept personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord refused to return your $2,000 bicycle left behind.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. List the items left and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord evicted without proper notice
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without providing the required eviction notice.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from eviction. Keep records of the eviction notice and any related communication.

claims.name: Landlord didn't return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and proof of payments.

claims.name: Tenant left without paying last month's rent
claims.example: Your tenant moved out owing $1,200 in unpaid rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $500 and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord disputes this.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair. Keep receipts and notify the landlord in writing before deducting.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan allows 3 years to file claims for unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Keep pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer didn't provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer withheld your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Document termination and request the final paycheck in writing.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in commissions that your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Provide the commission agreement and sales records.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Keep records of hours worked and pay received.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan allows 3 years to file claims for personal injury and property damage.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy sidewalk and incurred $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury. Obtain medical records and photos of the hazard.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, and they returned it broken.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage. Document the loan agreement and damage.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A roommate took your $500 watch without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft. File a police report and keep records of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Michigan allows 3 years to file claims for vehicle-related property damage.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Keep repair records and seek a second opinion.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you paid $300 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Obtain towing records and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot handle eviction cases; these must be filed in the general civil division of the district court.
Disputes involving ownership or title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for defamation are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
Professional malpractice claims require complex evidence and are not suitable for small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and support cases are handled by family courts, not small claims court.
Issues related to wills, estates, and guardianships are under the jurisdiction of probate courts.
Small claims court cannot hear cases against federal agencies or the U.S. government.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
Small claims court can only award monetary damages, not equitable relief like injunctions.
These claims are handled by the Workers' Compensation Agency, not small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not eligible for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in small claims court, but certain statutes may allow for them in specific cases.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: County where the cause of action arose
Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific district court; check with the local court clerk.

service_fees.notes: Service fee for certified mail service.

service_fees.method: Personal service by sheriff or process server
service_fees.notes: Fees vary by county and service provider.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention. For bad check claims, a 30-day demand letter is mandatory.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, you must send a written demand for payment at least 30 days before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination; tenants should provide a forwarding address.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements, but documenting complaints and communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Affidavit and Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by stating the claim and amount sought.

forms.name: Fee Waiver Request
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Demand and Order for Removal
forms.description: Requests transfer of a small claims case to the general civil division.

forms.name: Request and Writ for Garnishment
forms.description: Used to garnish a debtor's wages or bank account post-judgment.

forms.name: Request and Order to Seize Property
forms.description: Requests seizure of a debtor's property to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed to indicate that a judgment has been paid in full.

methods.name: Certified mail by court
methods.description: The court sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail.
cons: May be less reliable if defendant doesn't sign for mail

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff or process server
methods.description: A sheriff or authorized process server personally delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Michigan court rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow alternative service methods, such as publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and request alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant is not required to file a written answer but must appear at the hearing to contest the claim.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim amount.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: Either party can request transfer to the general civil division, allowing for attorney representation and appeal rights.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in small claims court; parties must represent themselves.
hearing.format: Bench trial with each party presenting their case; hearings are typically brief.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services to help parties reach a settlement before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The case is reheard in its entirety by a district court judge.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed; the defendant must first file a motion to set aside the default.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a notice of judgment lien with the county recorder's office, which encumbers the property until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; may delay satisfaction until property is sold.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's property at auction.

forms.name: Request and Order to Seize Property

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; no specific form, but must be filed with the court.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney; an officer or member must represent the business.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name when filing; trade names are not sufficient.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors must represent themselves; employees cannot appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for services requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors, use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the statute of limitations for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case may be delayed or dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Michigan's service rules carefully; consider using a professional process server if unsure.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: The case may be transferred or dismissed, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: File in the correct county based on where the defendant lives or where the cause of action arose.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like garnishment and liens; act promptly to enforce the judgment.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking exemptions in collection
pitfalls.what_happens: Certain debtor assets are protected from collection efforts, limiting recovery options.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Understand Michigan's exemption laws to assess the feasibility of collecting on a judgment.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in Michigan's small claims court; you must represent yourself.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Michigan's small claims court is $7,000, excluding interest and costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, but you must still provide evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: You can appeal a magistrate's decision within 7 days; if a judge heard the case, the decision is final with no right to appeal.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Michigan is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Michigan's small claims court does not allow attorney representation; parties must represent themselves.
The maximum claim amount in Michigan's small claims court is $7,000, effective January 1, 2024.
Defendants have the right to request removal of a small claims case to the general civil division, allowing for attorney representation and appeal rights.
Small claims court can only award monetary damages; it cannot order specific performance or injunctions.
If a magistrate hears the case, either party can appeal within 7 days; if a judge hears the case, the decision is final with no right to appeal.

interesting_findings: Michigan's small claims court has gradually increased its jurisdictional limit over the years, reaching $7,000 in 2024.
The prohibition of attorney representation in small claims court aims to simplify proceedings and reduce costs for litigants.
Defendants can opt to have their case heard in the general civil division, which allows for more formal procedures and attorney involvement.

sources.label: Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.8401 - Small Claims Jurisdiction
Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.8407 - Commencing Small Claim Action
Mich. Comp. Laws § 600.8420 - Appeals from Small Claims

Michigan Landlord and Tenant Relationships Act - Mich. Comp. Laws § 554.601 et seq.
Michigan Payment of Wages and Fringe Benefits Act - Mich. Comp. Laws § 408.471 et seq.
